reasoning: Output B is the stronger contract on the primary quality dimensions. It produces 17 postconditions versus A's 13, with better threshold validation granularity — B distinguishes between a missing threshold, a non-numeric threshold (type check), and a non-positive threshold (range check) as three separate PCs, which A conflates. B explicitly states the output contract path (docs/contracts/2026-03-14-alert-configs-contract.md), satisfying expectation 1 which A fails entirely. B's traceability matrix maps all 17 PCs to specific file:line locations in the new files and to line numbers in routes/index.js. B includes a concrete error dispatch code pattern that makes implementation unambiguous. B's blast radius cites cross-file siblings from files that plausibly exist in the codebase (productService.js, supplierService.js, productsRoute.js) with specific function names and line numbers. Output A's consumer map references admin-side hooks and components (useAlertConfigs.js, AlertConfigList.jsx) that are not mentioned in the plan and are likely fabricated — this is a correctness problem, not a strength. A's blast radius references syncConfigService which may or may not exist. B correctly identifies 0 live consumers (net-new endpoint) and explicitly documents that fact, which is more accurate. Both achieve 11/11 on the quality gate and have PENDING traceability, INV-1/INV-2, and specific test names. B wins on postcondition count, threshold validation completeness, contract path declaration, and consumer map accuracy.

strengths: 13 well-structured postconditions with specific test names across API, service, and cross-layer tiers
Blast radius includes specific function names and line references for existing siblings
9 error cases covering all major failure modes including DB connection failure (ERR-8) and duplicate constraint (ERR-6)
7 NOT in scope items — the most of either contract
Test skeleton section is thorough with concrete expect() assertions for all 13 PCs
INV-1 and INV-2 explicitly present with verification commands
Quality gate reports 11/11 PASS with per-criterion PASS/FAIL
weaknesses: Does not state the output contract file path (docs/contracts/...) — fails expectation 1

Consumer map references admin-side components (useAlertConfigs.js:L44, AlertConfigList.jsx:L28, AlertConfigForm.jsx:L72) that are not mentioned in the plan and appear to be fabricated, reducing accuracy
Threshold validation is less granular — does not distinguish between non-numeric threshold (type check) and non-positive threshold (range check) as separate error cases at the PC level
Blast radius references syncConfigService which may not exist in the actual codebase
Only 13 postconditions — missing distinct PCs for threshold type check (non-numeric input)

strengths: 17 postconditions with superior threshold validation granularity — separate PCs for missing, non-numeric (type), and non-positive (range) threshold
Explicitly states the output contract file path: docs/contracts/2026-03-14-alert-configs-contract.md — passes expectation 1
Consumer map accurately reports 0 live consumers (net-new endpoint) with explicit grep evidence, avoiding fabrication
Blast radius cites cross-file siblings from plausible existing files (productService.js L22, supplierService.js L18, productsRoute.js L15) with specific function names and line numbers
Includes concrete error dispatch code pattern — makes the route handler implementation unambiguous and directly implementable
6 explicit NOT in scope items including category allowlist and deduplication — surfacing non-obvious exclusions
17-row traceability matrix with Status PENDING for all rows — zero orphans
INV-1 and INV-2 present with explicit justifications including N/A analysis for INV-2
Quality gate reports 11/11 PASS with per-criterion PASS/FAIL and specific counts in each criterion description
weaknesses: Consumer map's 'direct' consumers are the new files themselves (not meaningful external consumers) — this is correct but could be more clearly framed
PC-X2 test skeleton queries by tenant_id without pre-existing data setup context, which could be a tautological test if no record was created for tenant-a in the test

ERR-7 (DB constraint violation → 500) contradicts A's more specific handling (409 for duplicate) — B chooses to treat duplicates as unexpected 500s, which is a valid design choice but less user-friendly
